# Packaging and sharing extensions {#sec:packages}

An extension can grow from one file to a directory and then to an npm or git package. Packages are delivery mechanisms for trusted extensions, skills, prompts, and themes; review their code before installing them.

## Distribute an extension as a Pi package

For a reusable package, declare explicit resources in `package.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "@acme/pi-team-tools",
  "keywords": ["pi-package"],
  "peerDependencies": {
    "@earendil-works/pi-ai": "*",
    "@earendil-works/pi-agent-core": "*",
    "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent": "*",
    "@earendil-works/pi-tui": "*",
    "typebox": "*"
  },
  "pi": {
    "extensions": ["./extensions"],
    "skills": ["./skills"],
    "prompts": ["./prompts"],
    "themes": ["./themes"]
  }
}
```

Put third-party runtime packages in `dependencies`, not `devDependencies`:
Pi package installation uses production installs by default (npm installs
without development dependencies). Core Pi packages
are bundled by Pi, so list each one the package actually imports as a
`peerDependency` with the documented `"*"` range rather than bundling a
competing copy. The broad manifest above assumes a full-featured package that
imports all five bundled packages. Include `@earendil-works/pi-tui` only when
code imports TUI components or TUI types for custom UI/rendering. Include
`typebox` only when code imports schemas directly from `typebox` rather than
using the `Type`
re-export from `@earendil-works/pi-ai`. Package conventions also discover
`extensions/`, `skills/`, `prompts/`, and `themes/` when a `pi` manifest is not
present, but an explicit manifest is clearer and supports filtering.

The `pi-package` keyword is an npm search and package-gallery convention, not
something Pi reads: Pi loads a package from its declared source and manifest,
never from keywords. Keep the keyword so others can find the package on
npmjs.com and in the community package gallery.

Users can install a package from npm or git with `pi install`, or list it under
the `packages` array in settings. Project-installed packages are trust-gated
just like project-local extensions.

### Step-by-step: from zero to a shareable extension package

The following example creates a minimal distributable package outside the Pi
monorepo.

**Step 1 -- Initialize a package**

```bash
mkdir pi-hello-demo
cd pi-hello-demo
npm init -y
```

**Step 2 -- Add the extension code**
```bash
mkdir extensions
```

Create `extensions/index.ts` with the hello-pi extension built in
[Your first extension](#sec:extensions).

**Step 3 -- Configure `package.json`**

Edit `package.json` to add:

```json
{
  "name": "@your-name/pi-hello-demo",
  "keywords": ["pi-package"],
  "peerDependencies": {
    "@earendil-works/pi-ai": "*",
    "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent": "*"
  },
  "pi": {
    "extensions": ["./extensions"]
  }
}
```

The `pi` manifest declares its resource directories, and `peerDependencies`
identifies the Pi runtime packages supplied by the host. This list is
intentionally shorter than the earlier generic manifest: the hello extension
imports only `@earendil-works/pi-ai` and `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`.
Add `@earendil-works/pi-agent-core`, `@earendil-works/pi-tui`, or `typebox`
only if the package imports them.

**Step 4 -- Test locally**

Install the package from its local path into a project directory where you use
Pi:

```bash
pi install /absolute/path/to/pi-hello-demo
```

A relative path such as `pi install ../pi-hello-demo` works as well: any
source that does not start with `npm:`, `git:`, `github:`, `http(s):`, or
`ssh:` is treated as a local path. Local-path packages are resolved in place,
not copied, so edits to the source are picked up on the next start or
`/reload`. The install command also adds the source to the `packages` array in
settings (global by default, project settings with `-l`), which you can
equivalently edit by hand.

Start Pi. Your extension should load. Test with `/hello-pi` and verify
the startup notification appears.

**Step 5 -- Publish to npm**

```bash
npm publish
```

Users can then install it with:

```bash
pi install npm:@your-name/pi-hello-demo
```

`pi install` also accepts git sources (`git:github.com/user/repo`,
`git:git@github.com:user/repo`, `https://github.com/user/repo`, or
`ssh://git@github.com/user/repo`) for testing before publishing.

## Manage installed packages

`pi list` prints the packages configured in user settings and, separately, in
project settings, each with its installed path; packages whose resources are
filtered by settings are marked `(filtered)`.

`pi remove <source>` removes a package and deletes its source from settings
(`pi uninstall` is an alias). Pass `-l` to remove from project settings
instead of global settings. The source argument must match the configured
source; if nothing matches, the command reports `No matching package found`.

`pi update` covers three targets:

- `pi update` with no arguments updates Pi itself (same as `pi update --self`
  or `pi update pi`). `--force` reinstalls even when the current version is
  the latest.
- `pi update --extensions` updates all installed packages;
  `pi update <source>` or `pi update --extension <source>` updates one.
- `pi update --models` refreshes the model catalogs only.
- `pi update --all` updates Pi and all packages.

Version pinning controls what an update touches. An npm source with an exact
version, such as `npm:@foo/bar@1.2.3`, is pinned: `pi update` skips it, and
resolution reinstalls it if the installed copy no longer satisfies the
configured version. A range such as `npm:@foo/bar@^1.2.0` updates to the
latest version within that range, and an unpinned source tracks the latest
release. For git sources, a `#ref` is the configured checkout target rather
than an update lock: an update reconciles the clone to the configured ref.
Local-path packages are never updated because they are resolved in place.
Pin an exact version for published packages whose updates you want to review
before they land.

## Resource delivery

Packages may ship skills, prompts, and themes alongside extension code. Their resource paths and package filters follow the settings scope; use explicit manifest paths when the layout is non-standard. A package is not a separate execution mode: its extension code has the same privileges as Pi.

### Package layout conventions

| Directory | Contents | Discovered automatically? |
|---|---|---|
| `extensions/` | One or more `.ts` extension factories | Yes, when no `pi` manifest |
| `skills/` | `.md` skill files | Yes, when no `pi` manifest |
| `prompts/` | Prompt template files | Yes, when no `pi` manifest |
| `themes/` | JSON theme files | Yes, when no `pi` manifest |

With an explicit `pi` manifest, Pi uses only the declared paths. This supports
non-standard locations and lets a package exclude conventional directories.

Before sharing, test the installed form, pin or review dependencies as appropriate, and document the trust boundary for project installs.
